shower.thought: # 35 Original Shower Thoughts
We spend the first half of our lives learning who we are, and the second half unlearning it.
The scariest part about getting older isn't death—it's realizing how little you actually know about the people you've known your whole life.
You can't remember most of your life, which means you've already forgotten the person you used to be multiple times over.
Boredom might be the only emotion we can't blame on anyone else.
We treat our bodies like they're going to last forever until suddenly we don't.
The people who make you feel most alive are usually the ones who can hurt you the worst.
You only realize a song is overplayed once you stop loving it.
Every conversation you have with someone could be your last one with them, but you almost never act like it.
We call it "staying in touch" but we're really just pretending the distance doesn't matter.
Time moves differently depending on whether you're happy or sad, which means our memories are all unreliable narrators.
The things that are easiest to do are almost never the things worth doing.
You'll never know if you're remembering something correctly or just remembering the last time you remembered it.
Most of our insecurities are based on assumptions about what other people think, and we rarely bother to check if we're right.
We're all just pretending to know what we're doing, except some people are better at the pretending part.
The internet made it possible to know everything except how to be okay.
Your worst day ever will eventually become a funny story, which is a strange kind of mercy.
People spend years building walls to protect themselves, then spend even more years wondering why they feel alone.
We fear commitment because freedom feels like potential, but potential is just another word for not deciding anything yet.
The real tragedy isn't that people leave—it's that we spend so much time preparing for it that we miss the time they're still here.

motivational.thought: The obstacle is not blocking your path—it is the path. — Ancient Proverb
Most of life isn't about having the right answers. It's about asking better questions. — Unknown
You cannot talk yourself into discipline, but you can build it one small decision at a time. — Unknown
The best time to plant a tree was 20 years ago. The second best time is today. — Chinese Proverb
Regret weighs more than effort ever will. — Unknown
Your future self is watching you right now. Make them proud. — Unknown
Don't wait for permission to become who you already are. — Unknown
The only way out is through. Every single time. — Unknown
You're not supposed to figure it all out before you begin. You figure it out as you go. — Unknown
Success is boring. It's just the result of being boring in your habits. — Unknown
The gap between who you are and who you want to be is not a problem—it's the entire point. — Unknown
Momentum is built in the margins. Those small, unwitnessed moments when nobody's looking. — Unknown
Your life doesn't need to look like anyone else's. It needs to look like intention. — Unknown
Fear is just information telling you what matters to you. Listen, then act anyway. — Unknown
You don't need to be fixed. You need to be activated. — Unknown
Every expert was once a beginner who decided that looking foolish was less important than getting better. — Unknown
The cost of doing the work is always less than the cost of carrying regret. — Unknown
Stop waiting for the right circumstances. Circumstances are never right. You make them right. — Unknown
What you're avoiding is probably what you need most. — Unknown
The person who says it's impossible is usually interrupted by someone doing it. — Ancient Proverb

darkjoke.thought:
I told my therapist I had a fear of abandonment.
She said, "Let's schedule your next session," then checked her watch.

My friend said he was going to start journaling to process his emotions.
I said, "That's great." He said, "Yeah, I've already filled three notebooks." I said, "With what?" He said, "Reasons I hate journaling."

I asked my dad why he never shows affection.
He said, "I do. I paid for your college." I said, "You didn't." He said, "Exactly."

My therapist asked if I was sleeping well.
I said, "Eight hours a night." She said, "That's good." I said, "Straight through work, yeah."

I went to a self-help seminar on confidence.
The instructor asked for volunteers. Nobody raised their hand. He said, "This is going well."

My girlfriend said I never listen to her.
At least that's what I think she said—I wasn't really paying attention.

I told my friend he needed to be more decisive.
He said, "You're right, I should change." I said, "Or shouldn't." He said, "Now I'm angry at you too."

My doctor said I needed to reduce stress.
I said, "Any suggestions?" He said, "Stop coming to these appointments."

I asked my boss if I could get a raise.
He laughed so hard he had to sit down. I said, "Is that a no?" He said, "I'm just trying to remember what your job actually is."

My friend said therapy changed his life.
I said, "That's wonderful." He said, "Yeah, now I understand exactly why everyone leaves me."

I told my wife she was being irrational.
She asked for an example. I said, "You're upset with me." She said, "And?" I said, "That's the example."

My coworker asked why I look so tired.
I said, "I have insomnia." He said, "Have you tried counting sheep?" I said, "Yeah, I got to 30,000 and realized they're all dead."

I asked my friend if he believed in second chances.
He said, "Why?" I said, "No reason, just wondering." He said, "You're about to ask me for one, aren't you?" I said, "Third, actually."

My therapist told me I have commitment issues.
I said, "That's not true." She said, "You've changed therapists four times this year." I said, "I'm still thinking about it."

I ran into my ex at the grocery store.
She said, "You look well." I said, "So do you." She said, "I meant compared to how depressed you'll be when I leave again."

My friend said he was going through an existential crisis.
I said, "Welcome to Tuesday." He said, "Does it get better?" I said, "No, you just get better at ignoring it."

I asked my barber if he ever got tired of listening to people's problems.
He said, "Constantly." I said, "Why do you stay?" He said, "Insurance benefits and the knowledge that people will always be this miserable."

My mom asked if I was happy.
I said, "Sure." She said, "You don't sound sure." I said, "Now I'm not sure if I was supposed to sound sure or be actually sure." She said, "See? This is why your father left."

I told my friend he had unrealistic standards for relationships.
He said, "Like what?" I said, "You want someone who actually likes you." He said, "Is that unrealistic?" I said, "In your case, yeah."

I asked my accountant if I was saving enough for retirement.
He said, "Are you planning to retire?" I said, "Eventually." He said, "Then no."
